posts.title: New Dads: Start the Bonding Now — Before Birth
posts.excerpt: Early bonding doesn't have to wait until birth — in fact, the research suggests it should start in the womb. Evidence shows that babies can recognise their father's voice before they're even born.

body.text: Early bonding doesn't have to wait until birth — in fact, the research suggests it should start in the womb. This might sound surprising, but the science is clear: your baby is listening long before they arrive.
What the research tells us

Evidence shows that in the womb, babies can recognise voices they've heard regularly. A 2013 study published in the journal Acta Paediatrica found that newborns showed measurable responses to voices they had heard prenatally — including their father's.
This means that those early bonds are already forming, giving both of you a head start before the first nappy is changed.
Simple things you can do now

items: Talk to your partner's bump daily — read aloud, describe your day, or simply say hello
Sing or hum — it doesn't matter if you're not in tune
Attend scans and midwife appointments — involvement matters
Learn about what's happening developmentally week by week

body.text: You don't need to do anything elaborate. Consistency and presence are what count.
Read the full research paper

posts.title: Is My Child's Speech Delayed? What the Research Says
posts.excerpt: Understanding typical speech milestones and when to seek support — a clear, evidence-based guide for parents navigating this common concern.

body.text: One of the most common worries parents bring to health visitors is whether their child's speech is developing as it should. The good news is that there's a wide range of what's considered typical — and knowing the milestones helps you ask the right questions.
Key milestones by age

items: 12 months: Babbling, says 1–3 words, responds to their name
18 months: 10–20 words, points to things they want
2 years: 50+ words, starting to combine two words
3 years: Short sentences, strangers can understand most of what they say

body.text: When to seek support

If your child isn't meeting milestones, or if you simply have a gut feeling something isn't quite right, trust that instinct and speak to your GP or health visitor. Early intervention makes a significant difference.
Speech and Language Therapy referrals can be made through your GP, health visitor, or self-referral in many areas.

posts.title: Parental Burnout Is Real — Here's How to Spot It Early
posts.excerpt: The signs, the science, and the small daily practices that make a meaningful difference to your mental health as a parent.

body.text: Parental burnout is a state of chronic exhaustion that goes beyond the normal tiredness of having children. It was formally identified in research by Isabelle Roskam and Moïra Mikolajczak, and it's more common than most parents realise — or admit.
The four signs to watch for

items: Overwhelming exhaustion related specifically to your parenting role
Emotional distancing — going through the motions without feeling present
Loss of the pleasure and fulfilment you once found in parenting
Contrast with how you felt before — a sense of who you used to be as a parent

body.text: What actually helps

The research is clear that two things make the biggest difference: reducing the demands on you, and increasing your resources. That means accepting help, protecting sleep where possible, and having honest conversations with your partner or support network.
Small things matter too: five minutes of quiet, a walk without the buggy, or simply naming how you feel to someone who will listen.

posts.title: Choosing a Primary School: What Really Matters Beyond Ofsted
posts.excerpt: Beyond the headline rating — the questions every parent should ask before choosing a school for their child.

body.text: An Ofsted rating is a snapshot, not a guarantee. Outstanding schools can coast; schools rated Good can be exceptional. What matters most is whether a school is the right fit for your specific child.
Questions worth asking on an open day

items: How do you support children who learn differently?
What does a typical day look like for a child in Year 1?
How do you communicate with parents when something isn't working?
What is your approach to behaviour — and what does that look like in practice?
How much time do children spend outside?

body.text: Trust what you observe
Walk the corridors. Are the children's faces relaxed? Do staff greet children warmly? Is there noise — the productive, engaged kind — or is it unnaturally quiet? Your instincts as a parent are data.

posts.title: The Power of Pretend Play — and Why It's Non-Negotiable
posts.excerpt: Play is how children make sense of the world. Here's what the evidence says about prioritising it even in busy lives.

body.text: When your three-year-old insists that the sofa is a pirate ship and you are the sea monster, they are not simply being imaginative. They are doing some of the most cognitively demanding work of their young lives.
What play actually develops

items: Executive function — planning, flexibility, and impulse control
Language and vocabulary through narrative and role play
Emotional regulation by rehearsing difficult scenarios safely
Social skills: negotiation, turn-taking, reading others' cues

body.text: Dr Stuart Brown's decades of research concludes that play is not a break from learning — it is the primary mechanism through which children learn.
What this means for busy parents
You don't need to engineer elaborate activities. The most valuable play is unstructured and child-led. Your job is to protect the time and space for it to happen — and to join in when invited.

posts.title: Why Your Toddler's Tantrums Are a Sign of Good Development
posts.excerpt: The neuroscience of emotional regulation — and practical strategies that actually work when you're in the thick of it.

body.text: The tantrum is one of parenting's most testing moments — and also one of the most developmentally significant. Understanding what's happening in your child's brain during a meltdown changes everything about how you respond.
The neuroscience in plain English

The prefrontal cortex — the part of the brain responsible for emotional regulation, rational thought, and impulse control — is not fully developed until the mid-twenties. Your toddler is not being manipulative. They literally cannot regulate big emotions yet.
Dr Daniel Siegel calls this 'flipping the lid' — when the emotional brain overwhelms the thinking brain entirely. Co-regulation (your calm presence) is what helps them come back down.
What actually helps in the moment

items: Stay calm yourself — your nervous system regulates theirs
Get down to their level physically
Name the emotion: 'You're really frustrated right now'
Don't try to reason until they've calmed — the thinking brain is offline
Offer comfort without giving in to the original demand
